questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
questions.subsection: Line of symmetry for regular polygons
questions.structural_anchor: Line of symmetry for regular polygons
questions.question: How many lines of symmetry does a regular decagon have?
questions.hint: For a regular polygon, the number of symmetry lines equals the number of sides.
questions.explanation: A regular decagon has 10 sides, so it has 10 lines of symmetry. Option A is for pentagon; B for octagon; D would be for dodecagon.

calculation_steps: Regular decagon n=10 → 10 lines of symmetry

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Line of symmetry for regular polygons
source_trace.structural_element: Line of symmetry for regular polygons
source_trace.content_anchor: n-sided regular polygon has n lines of symmetry

explanations_tiered.definition: Regular n-gon has n lines of symmetry.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Decagon has n=10 → 10 lines of symmetry.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A regular 10-sided shape can be folded in 10 different ways so both sides match.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The number of symmetry lines equals the number of sides for any regular polygon.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Dihedral group D10 contains 10 reflection symmetries.
reasoning_steps: Recall property: lines of symmetry = n.
misconceptions: Confusing with rotational symmetry order (also n).

flashcard.front: Lines of symmetry in regular decagon?

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Regular decagon has 10 lines of symmetry.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.2 Sum of Exterior Angles of a Convex Polygons
questions.structural_anchor: Sum of the exterior angles of a polygon
questions.question: A convex polygon has each exterior angle measuring \(12^\circ\). What is the sum of the exterior angles?
questions.hint: The sum of exterior angles is constant.
questions.explanation: The sum of exterior angles (one per vertex) of any convex polygon is always \(360^\circ\), regardless of each angle's measure. Option D would be the product of 12 and 360, incorrect.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.2 Sum of Exterior Angles of a Convex Polygons
source_trace.structural_element: Sum of the exterior angles of a polygon

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The sum does not depend on each angle's size; it's always 360°.
explanations_tiered.beginner: No matter what each exterior angle measures, all of them together always add up to 360°.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The constant sum of exterior angles is 360°, independent of n.
misconceptions: Multiplying 12 by n (n=30) to get 360, then thinking sum depends on n.

publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: find the measure of each interior or exterior angle of a regular polygon
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Sum of exterior angles of any convex polygon is always 360°.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
questions.subsection: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle
questions.question: A regular pentagon is inscribed in a circle of radius 5 cm. Using \( \sin 36^\circ = 0.5878 \), find its perimeter to one decimal place.
questions.explanation: \(P = 2 \times 5 \times 5 \times \sin 36^\circ = 50 \times 0.5878 = 29.39 \approx 29.4\text{ cm}\). Option B would be if using r=5 but n=4; C too high.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle

explanations_tiered.beginner: First compute 2×5×5=50, then multiply by sin36° (0.5878) to get 29.39, round to 29.4.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Chord length formula applied: each side = 2r sin(36°), times 5 sides.

publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: solve problems on area and perimeter of regular polygons

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.3 Measures of Each Interior Angle and Exterior Angle of a Regular Polygon
questions.question: A regular polygon has each interior angle measuring \(162^\circ\). What is the measure of each exterior angle?
questions.explanation: Exterior = \(180^\circ - 162^\circ = 18^\circ\). Option B would be for interior 160°, C for 158°, D for 156°.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.3 Measures of Each Interior Angle and Exterior Angle of a Regular Polygon

explanations_tiered.beginner: Straight line is 180°, so subtract interior from 180 to get exterior: 180 - 162 = 18°.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Linear pair relationship at each vertex.

publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: find the measure of each interior or exterior angle of a regular polygon

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
questions.question: What is the central angle of a regular nonagon (9 sides)?
questions.explanation: For n=9, central angle = \(360^\circ / 9 = 40^\circ\). Option B is for decagon, D for octagon, A for dodecagon? 30° would be 12 sides.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon

explanations_tiered.advanced: Each central angle subtends one side.

publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: find the measure of each interior or exterior angle of a regular polygon

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.1 Sum of Interior Angles of a Convex Polygons
questions.structural_anchor: Deriving sum of the interior angles of a polygon
questions.question: The sum of the interior angles of a convex polygon is \(1980^\circ\). How many sides does it have?

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.1 Sum of Interior Angles of a Convex Polygons
source_trace.structural_element: Deriving sum of the interior angles of a polygon

explanations_tiered.beginner: Divide 1980 by 180 to get 11, then add 2 to get 13 sides.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
questions.subsection: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle
questions.question: A regular octagon is inscribed in a circle of radius 6 cm. Using \( \sin 22.5^\circ = 0.3827 \), find its perimeter to one decimal place.
questions.explanation: \(P = 2 \times 8 \times 6 \times \sin 22.5^\circ = 96 \times 0.3827 = 36.7392 \approx 36.7\text{ cm}\). Option A would be for n=6?; C too high; D for n=12?

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What is the term for the circle that passes through all vertices of a regular polygon?
questions.explanation: The circumcircle passes through all vertices. Incircle (A) touches sides; apothem (C) is a segment; escribed circle is outside.

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The circumscribed circle passes through each vertex of the polygon.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A circle that goes through every corner of a polygon is the circumcircle.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The circumcircle has its center at the polygon's center and radius to vertices.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Circumradius R = distance from center to vertex.
reasoning_steps: Recall circle that passes through vertices = circumcircle.

glossary_entry.definition: Circle through all vertices of a polygon.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Circle through all vertices of a polygon.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Circumcircle passes through all vertices; incircle touches sides.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.2 Sum of Exterior Angles of a Convex Polygons
questions.structural_anchor: Sum of the exterior angles of a polygon
questions.question: A student argues: 'If a polygon has more sides, each exterior angle must be smaller, so the sum of exterior angles decreases.' Why is this reasoning incorrect?

options.A: Each exterior angle increases with more sides.
options.B: The sum of exterior angles is always \(360^\circ\), independent of \(n\).
options.C: More sides cause the sum to increase.

questions.hint: The total turn around the polygon is always one full circle.
questions.explanation: While each exterior angle does decrease as \(n\) increases, the sum remains constant at \(360^\circ\). Option B correctly states the constant sum property; the others are false.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.2 Sum of Exterior Angles of a Convex Polygons
source_trace.structural_element: Sum of the exterior angles of a polygon

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Although each exterior angle gets smaller, the number of angles increases, keeping the product (sum) constant at 360°.
explanations_tiered.beginner: More sides mean more turns, but each turn is smaller. The total turn is still 360°.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Each exterior = 360/n, sum = n × (360/n) = 360. So sum does not change with n.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Algebraic cancellation shows sum independent of n.
misconceptions: Assuming each angle smaller means sum smaller.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Sum constant 360° despite smaller individual angles.

flashcard.front: Why doesn't sum of exteriors decrease with more sides?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why doesn't exterior sum decrease with more sides?
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: find the measure of each interior or exterior angle of a regular polygon
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The sum of exterior angles is constant at 360°, so the student's reasoning is incorrect.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
questions.subsection: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle
questions.structural_anchor: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle
questions.question: For a regular hexagon inscribed in a circle of radius \(r\), the apothem is \(a = r \cos 30^\circ\). Which of the following correctly expresses the area in terms of \(r\)?
questions.explanation: \(a = r \cos 30^\circ = r \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\), \(P = 6r\). Area = \(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}r \times 6r = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} r^2\). Option D expands to same but not simplified; C is standard.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.4 Properties of Regular Polygons: pentagon, hexagon, octagon and decagon
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle
source_trace.structural_element: Perimeter, area and apothem of regular polygons inscribed in a circle

explanations_tiered.beginner: Area = half times apothem times perimeter. Plug in a = r cos30° and P=6r, simplify to (3√3/2)r².

flashcard.front: Hexagon area in terms of r?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Hexagon area in terms of circumradius r?
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: solve problems on area and perimeter of regular polygons
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Area = ½ × a × P = ½ × (r√3/2) × 6r = (3√3/2)r².
